intro: Nebuchadnezzar II (Babylonian Nabu-kudurri-usur, meaning "Nebo, protect the crown"), son of Nabopolassar, was the greatest king of the Neo-Babylonian Empire (reigned c. 605–562 B.C.) and the central foreign power in the books of Daniel, Jeremiah, and Kings. He defeated Pharaoh Necho at Carchemish (605 B.C.), subjugated Judah, and carried the first deportation to Babylon (Dan. 1:1–2). After Jehoiakim's rebellion he took the second deportation in 597 B.C. including Jehoiachin, and after Zedekiah's revolt he besieged Jerusalem for 18 months, destroyed Solomon's temple in 586 B.C., and carried the bulk of the remaining population into exile. God called him "my servant" as an instrument of judgment (Jer. 27:6). His pride was humbled in the episode of Daniel 4, where he was driven to eat grass like an ox until he acknowledged that God rules over the kingdoms of men.
